segment-text: Will all members of the Gala Committee - and remember that includes you, Cissy - please note that there's an emergency meeting at twelve noon sharp! I don't use the adjective, "emergency," lightly. I will start as the hour strikes.
He totters out on his ivory cane motioning to BOBBY to follow him. Much chatter.
Don't forget to remind me, ReggieREGGIE I'll set the alarm.

As REGGIE sets his watch, OCTAVIA starts to sing. Her voice is remarkably strong but at one point, she forgets the words, and substitutes "Dee-dee-dee-dee." She stops, embarrassed. The other singers remain respectful.
INT. DINING ROOM - MORNING

The residents are eating their breakfasts. Some are being served coffee. DWAYNE doles out pills to various RESIDENTS. SHERYL, sits next to OCTAVIA. Two Polish waitresses, MARTA and EVA, chat in Polish to each other as they clear. They start to clear the plate of DAPHNE MORGAN who is finishing her porridge. At her table are TONY ROSE and DAVE.
Wait, wait! (takes another two mouthfuls then pushes the bowl towards them) Thank you.

EVA and MARTA, unphased, continue chatting in Polish and move on to other tables, passing ANNE LANGLEY and then GEORGE, Chorus. GEORGE is observing an argument going on at a table next to a window where REGGIE and CISSY are sitting, her earphones by her side. At the only other table by the window sits CEDRIC, LETITIA, and BOBBY. CISSY glances out the window. It's the chorus vs. the soloists. HARRY is standing between REGGIE and an empty chair (WILF'S). HARRY makes a few attempts to move the chair to seat himself, but REGGIE holds firmly with one hand and one leg, as he continues to do The Telegraph crossword. The following
REGGIE ignores GEORGE, holding fast to the chair with one leg and one hand while he continues to do the crossword.

(trying to pull the seat) You soloists come in and just sing a few arias. We sing the entire bloody opera. (pulls back) You may be on stage the entire time, but you're not actually singing, you're just standing in the back.
HARRY begins to argue back and REGGIE overrides him.
(indicates people at his table) Why now, Harry? We've been sitting at this table for as long as I've been here.
WILF has entered the dining room, supported by his cane, waving to others as he goes toward his table.
Exactly my point! If this place shuts down in six months, which it bloody well could (he looks at others in the room) I want to be able to look back and remember I had a fucking window seat!
As WILF reaches the table, a few cheers from GEORGE and other chorus residents seated at their tables.
He moves the empty chair over and sits in it with difficulty, holding his cane. HARRY goes back to his table, sits, starts to take his pills.
I could have had a window seat, but I much prefer to sit by myself.
CISSY, a bit unnerved, puts her earphones on.
You can't deny it, Wilf, soloists would be nothing without us.
If I hear that again, Harry, I may stick a fork up your jacksy.
(sitting next to HARRY) He's right, Wilf.
Anybody got a fork?
You know he isWILF Make that two forks. ANGELIQUE, 20, French, has brought WILF a full English
breakfast. He is getting his usual special treatment from ANGELIQUE as other plates are getting cleared.
Merci, Angelique. Have you done something new to your hair?
No. It's the same. I just put
fringe in front.
I love your fringe in front.
REGGIE looks up from his paper.
Extra slice fried bread this morning and your favourite(places jam on his tray) - apricot Jam.
REGGIE lowers his paper and looks steelily at ANGELIQUE.
Dr. Cogan she say too much of the sugar is bad for you, but I know you like very much. Same for Madame Langley.
With a wink, she nods in the direction of ANNE LANGLEY, who sits at a nearby table eating delicately.
You're an angel, Angelique. C'est magnifique. Vous fancy a rumpypumpy cette soiree, Angelique?
Qu'est que c'est "rumpy pumpy"?
Angelique, are we getting married or not? I need your answer.
(used to it) Bon appetit!
She goes. REGGIE lowers his paper, stares at her. CISSY looks out of the window. CEDRIC, at the next table, leans over and taps her. She removes her earphones and looks at CEDRIC.
Ready.
Cissy, remember, the emergency meeting is at twelve noon. Precisely.
CISSY nods, turns back, puts earphones on.
(turns to CEDRIC)
Cedric, it's breakfast time. CEDRIC motions towards CISSY's back. He rotates his finger indicating CISSY is unbalanced.
Cedric! Chill! Hullo or
bonjour, look He points to the window. DR. COGAN is speaking urgently to TADEK, as they walk past the window. TADEK carries a window squeegee and a cloth. WILF watches them.
EXT. DRIVE, BEECHAM HOUSE - WILF'S POV - DAY
A people carrier is in the driveway. On the side of the vehicle:
BEECHAM HOUSE HOME FOR RETIRED MUSICIANS
DR. COGAN leaves. TADEK cleans the windows. He gets in the van and starts it.
INT. DINING ROOM - DAY
All looking out the window.
(to REGGIE) Didn't you say today's the day?
What's today?
(to CISSY) Tadek setting off to fetch the new arrival.
At least now we'll know who it is.
(excited) What? What? Is there to be a new arrival?
Cissy, we've been talking about it for days.
Have I? Have we? Then who is it? How do you know it's today?
They'd only send the van for a star.
They sent a private car with a chauffeur for me.
I was thinking of giving my Figaro.
You must let me know in plenty of time.
Felicity dear, I'm the pianist. I'm the one who plays the score. You're the one who turns the pages.
FELICITY and LETITIA walk away.
I'm still a professional and I can't bear it when you're annoyed with me.
CISSY stops at LOTTE'S table.
I didn't think you were out of tune at all.
Anne, what are you going to do for the gala?
What?
She continues to eat.
What are you going to do for the gala?
She spontaneously begins to sing an aria from TOSCA Vissi D'Arte. !"#$%&'()*+,&./%0122"13%4/../%5/679/::;%"%<4=7>?
!"#$3%/K%.F7%6F<L:%OLN.3%F/O>LKP%<%:7H/.7%6/K.:/O3% WILF dismounts the chair lift.
Go for your walk, Wilfred.
I'd love it if you could join me, Lucy.
Go for your walk.
(passing her) I must tell you Lucy, it's hard to leave you.
(continues arranging flowers) Do your best. May I?
WILF picks a flower and tries to put it in his lapel.
I can't see it without my glasses. Would you be so kind?
WILF bends while DR. COGAN goes to fit the flower in his lapel. * Their faces close. He grins at her.
You don't have a lapel, Wilf.
What do you know?
Go for your walk, Wilf.
EXT. BEECHAM HOUSE & GROUNDS - DAY

Other residents and their visitors also taking the air, greeting each other. WILF waves. Some wave back. From a nearby window, the muffled, intermittent sound of "Underneath the Arches" accompanied by piano and trumpet. Four Gilbert and Sullivan singers are playing croquet.
A pound says I'll kick your arse, Nigel.
The way you play, I'm sure you will.
(continues to walk) And watch out, Nobby, Cissy Robson's been looking for you all morning. Can't think why, I'm so much more attractive.
WILF walks on.
(calls out) Says you. (beat, shouts after WILF) How's the leg?
Which one?
EXT. TERRACE - DAY On the terrace, tables and chairs under parasols.

Residents reading, TONY and DAVE are playing a game of draughts. REGGIE sits at a table in a shaded corner, writing, preparing his lecture - A BIOGRAPHY OF VERDI and The Telegraph are on the table next to him. CISSY, seated next to REGGIE, listens to her CD while watching TWO LITTLE GIRLS (one of them is ESME) and a BOY playing on the grass. LETITIA, sitting on the other side of the terrace, with her DAUGHTER, chat while watching their kids. WILF approaches, goes to sit next to REGGIE and CISSY. REGGIE glances up and continues writing.
(as he sits) I read somewhere that the average man thinks about sex every seven seconds.
(writing) And do you?
(gazing at CISSY as she sits) I wish it was only every seven seconds.
You need to see a psychiatrist.
Male or female?
REGGIE continues to write.

(picking up a newspaper on the table, pulls his glasses on, looks at the crossword puzzle) You left a word out. (states the clue - and he gets it, pauses, he studies CISSY, takes his glasses off) When I first saw her, God knows how long ago, I thought she was the most beautiful, sexiest thing I'd ever clapped eyes on.
The alarm on REGGIE'S wristwatch sounds. He is about to tap CISSY on the arm but WILF stops him with a look.

Cissy, let Reg and me have our way with you now. Go on, be a sport, on the lawn, legs apart, bloomers abandoned. I'll remove my truss. (taps her on the arm with his cane, she removes her headset and looks at WILF) How about it?
Ready.
WILF loves it. REGGIE is forced to smile.
What are you laughing at?
WILF bends down to pick up the CD cover, but ends up sitting on the ground. He looks at the CD cover.
(still reading and making notes) I'm glad they've reissued it. We might get royalties.
WILF tries vainly to get up.
I wish they'd reissue us instead.
REGGIE helps him to his feet.
They might have put our photographs on the cover instead of just our names.
WILF studies the CD cover. In bold letters against an appropriate background: CLASSIC RECORDINGS - Jean HORTON sings GILDA with Reginald PAGET, Cecily ROBSON, Wilfred BOND in RIGOLETTO

Dizzy heights, those days. (sits) 'Jean Horton sings Gilda.' I should have had top billing. Name part, after all. (looks off) I remember the production, the recording sessions, everything, as if it were yesterday yet I can't remember what we had for breakfast this morningREGGIE (quietly) - fucking apricot jam, that's what you had.

(looks at CD again) Everyone said the quartet had never been better sung. My picture in the Daily Mail with the caption: "Wilfred Bond - A New Star?" Question mark. I was famous for a day. Still, I made a living and that's all I ever wanted. I'm not like you, Reg. You're an artist, I'm an artisan -
Have you listened to it?
No. Have you?
No, no way, Wilf. No way.
Of course. Clumsy of me.
These two old friends share a moment. REGGIE writes again. CISSY returns, panicked.
Have you seen my CD case?
WILF hands it to her. She rushes away as SIMON walks by.
(to SIMON - a rap line from Tinie Tempah's "Pass Out") "I'm born famous I'm sorta' known"
"If your son doesn't"
"I bet your daughter knows"
WILF and SIMON put their fists in the air. REGGIE makes a face.
Reg, you might as well get used to it. Rap is here to stay.
Why? Rap is not music.
It is to them.
Just in time, REGGIE sees WILF'S foot move his own ball towards the hoop for an easier shot.
(off-hand, not taking his eye off his ball) You just moved your ball.
Me? Unthinkable REGGIE I saw you WILF
I clearly just saw you move it WILF has been watching an attractive female visitor walking by with her husband.
(eyes following the woman) Balls! Precisely. Wilf, she's married.
Precisely. (he quotes a phrase from an Opera in Italian)
They play on and are enthusiastic about the prospect of performing.
Gilda! Our Gilda!
Oh! G, Gilda, yes, I see, our "Gilda," Rigoletto. You mean Jean Horton-
Yes! How clever of you! Yes, Jean Horton.
Jean Horton? Here? (he and CISSY look at REGGIE) You all right, old son?
REGGIE is frozen.
If it's any consolation, Reg, she's aged terribly. She looks
a hundredBrief, tense silence. Steady, Reg, steady, NSP, NSP REGGIE walks away.
It'll be all right, old son,
trust me REGGIE throws down his mallet and marches off towards the house. WILF and CISSY watch him, deeply concerned.
INT. DR. COGAN'S OFFICE - DAY
DR. COGAN, standing beside a table getting coffee. REGGIE is standing.
We'll have you looking as you always did, youthful and whining (corrects herself quickly) Shining. Youthful and shining -
In the mirror JEAN watches CISSY.
(a trace of an affectionate smile) You certainly haven't changed. I asked you where Reggie is.
JEAN, through the window, sees a COACH pulling up. School children start to get out.
Who are those children?
(looking) Oh, Reggie must be giving one of his talks today. (noticing the tray with orange juice) Did they serve you breakfast in your room?
Of course they did.
INT. MAIN HALL LANDING/STAIRCASE/STAIRCASE HALL - DAY
CISSY and JEAN walk from JEAN'S ROOM.

...what with people being sent away if they're too ill or go completely doolally, of course one gets depressed from time to time - I mean, I've woken up with the black dog on my shoulder a couple of times - but it never lasts for long. How could it? You'll see, we all look after each other and
that's very cheering JEAN and CISSY walk on. JEAN and CISSY are near the bottom of the stairs. BOBBY, in the background, waits.
(puzzled by the note) He didn't seem to recognise me. How odd.
His sight isn't what it was.
CISSY and JEAN walk on.
Such an intense little man. He
always looks as though he's
What does the note say?
Birthday Benefit Gala. Emergency. Top Secret.
Birthday benefit gala? Whose birthday? Mine isn't until the 6th of January?
(re-reading the note, puzzled) No, no, not yours, Jean, Giuseppe Verdi's. There's a Benefit Gala. Everyone's expected to perform to save our
What do you mean, perform?
INT. SMALL MUSIC ROOM - DAY
They walk toward the SMALL MUSIC ROOM.
We have to do what we do, Jean.
At our age? Ridiculous!
Everyone does something.
I won't sing at Verdi's gala or anyone else's. And that's final.
(admiring the deer) That's a beauty. I don't want to talk.
Of course not.
I really can do without this at my time of life.
(pause) Do you want me to go?
(quietly) No.
WILF produces the whiskey and two plastic cups.
(a ritual) Shall we?
REGGIE observes, but says nothing.
EXT. GARDEN & HOUSE - DAY
JEAN and CISSY are strolling. Now and then CISSY steals a glance at JEAN, monitoring her sadness.
Don't worry darling, I know where
the boys are. They walk.
Shall we sit for a moment? (gestures to a bench by the rose bed; they sit, she looks around)
A few residents with visitors, among them OLLY slowly bouncing his GRANDDAUGHTER, DAISY, on his shoulders. JEAN and CISSY catch the tail end of OLLY'S pony-ride. OLLY begins to put her down.
No Grandpa! One more!
OLLY struggles to put her back on his shoulders.
Oh, look, there's Olly with his grandchildren. (calling) Daisy, sweetheart!
OLLY gently puts DAISY on the ground, who runs over and gives CISSY a big hug.
EXT. SUMMERHOUSE - LATE AFTERNOON
REGGIE and WILF are sitting inside holding plastic cups containing whiskey. They have been drinking for awhile. REGGIE is in the process of refilling his.
So, come on, old son, break into song.
REGGIE smiles. He's been talking about aging.

...and in that way, we're not the same. I'm not like you, Wilf. I positively liked getting old. Physical decay has seemed natural and inescapable. I think I can say I've welcomed the inevitable cheerfully. I've made the
transition from opera star to
Then Jean arrives...
I remember thinking when you two got married...she was much more ambitious than you-
Of course she was. She was more ambitious than all of us.
-if I know anything about it. What can you know about it?
I was your best man, Reggie. Yes, and then you were married to the same woman for thirty thousand years. Call me old fashioned but did she know how often you were unfaithful to her?

You say some very harsh things, Reg. (gets up, a little unsteady.) I think I'll have myself a widdle. (looks around for the right tree, a little tipsy) You're right, Reg. I hate growing old. Hate every bloody moment of it. If it's not your prostate, it's piles. And then it's peeing five times a night. If you're lucky.
WILF steps off the Summer House and stumbles to a nearby tree. He unzips and begins to pee when he catches a glimpse of CISSY and JEAN walking nearby and sees that they have spotted him.
(shouts as he is peeing) You may want to avert your gaze, ladies. This may take a while. A wise man goes when he can, girls. A fool goes when he must.
He looks up to see REGGIE still in the Summer House, standing at the edge, wanting to flee.
WILF (CONT'D)
Easy, Reg, easyBy this time, CISSY and JEAN are near enough for JEAN to see REGGIE as he indelicately climbs down from the Summer House, takes a few steps, his back to them.
I've changed my mind. I don't think it's sophisticated enough for the gala.
We've been at it for a month.
What do you mean, it's not sophisticated enough?
Exactly what I say.
GEORGE and HARRY start to leave, GEORGE turns GEORGE Sorry, Cedric. I just want to be
We can't find any record of it.
(flustered) I didn't say I sang Giulio CHsare, I said I was considered for it.
A giggle or two from eavesdropping residents seated in the music room.
Right. Well you've considered us and, unlike you, we're singing it.
Yeah, we're singing it.
GEORGE and HARRY leave. NIGEL, baritone, belts out a brief phrase or two from Julius Caesar. The title role. He finishes.
Giulio CH-sare. Act 1, Scene 2. CEDRIC, trying to save face, looks at CISSY.
(looks sharply at CISSY) Yes, what is it?
What happened?
We started a half-hour ago. You're late.
(cheerfully) Well, here I am.
LETITIA raises her eyes to heaven.
(to LETITIA) What are you looking at?
What are you talking about?
I thought she saw something. I was afraid we were leaking again.
Have you asked Reggie to sing La donna e mobile at the gala?
Oh God, I forgot!
Good. Don't. For once, your forgetting, has paid dividends. I have a brilliant idea. ) ) ) ) )
Unfortunately, art is a luxury to be paid for by those to whom it matters.
Silence. CEDRIC senses the hook is set.
Well, I must leave. At any rate, I appreciate your consideration.
CEDRIC leaves.
(trying to be off-hand) It's one gala, Reg. You could do it. You could persuade Jean. I know it.
(looks at WILF, says wryly) Well, money has always driven art.
He takes out his notebook and pen.

One gala. It's our life. (as he's writing) Count Kaiserling commissioned the Goldberg Variations. Culture wasn't spread by artists but by merchants (hands the note to CISSY) It's to save Beecham House.
Well done, Reg.
Thank you, dear, dear, Reggie.
EXT. THE SWAN - NIGHT
JEAN marches from the pub. NOBBY exits the van and stands by the car door. D'you have a good time?
No. Open the door.
INT. THE SWAN - NIGHT
After a moment, WILF, REGGIE and CISSY begin to walk unsteadily through the restaurant. WILF tries to pick a flower from a vase and ends up toppling the vase, spilling the water.
(walking away) I think I spilled.

WILF goes to leave, CISSY is ahead. They pass a television in * the bar. CISSY continues on but something catches WILF and REGGIE'S eyes and they stop: A RAP GROUP singing but there is, of course, no sound.
What d'you think they're singing, Reg?
After a moment: Il quartetto da Rigoletto. Angle from the back of WILF and REGGIE - we see WILF is gesturing, imitating the rap group.
EXT. THE SWAN - NIGHT
JEAN stares through the van window as WILF and REGGIE start to stagger out of the restaurant.

(raps in an English accent) ...and I'm gonna live to be a hundred, And I can tell you why, (measures up with his arm) 'Cos I can still get it up - oh so high! (gestures with his arm, but much lower) He can still get it up - but not so high.
(laughing) Yo, yo, oh so high.
(calling from the van) Sorry to interrupt Gents, but Miss Horton would like to go back.
The van's engine starts.
(laughing) Yo, yo, yo, not so high.
WILF, REGGIE and CISSY stagger to the people carrier and
INT. PEOPLE CARRIER (TRAVELLING) - NIGHT
JEAN sits on the right side, staring out of the window. On the left side, REGGIE is staring out of his window, trying to stay awake. WILF and CISSY are asleep.
INT. JEAN'S ROOM - NIGHT - RAIN

33 RPM record coming out of sleeve and onto a turntable. Close up of the needle hitting the record. JEAN is silhouetted, sitting, listening, following almost soundlessly to her aria from Madama Butterfly, "Un Bel Di Vedremo."
INT. DINING ROOM - NEXT DAY BREAKFAST, MORNING
JEAN enters the dining room in her dressing gown and walks up to the table.
It's your decision. Now, let's see(scanning the score) You'll each need at least six sessions Panic: Six?
We don't have the time They look up to see NOBBY come into the room. He hands the note to REGGIE as he whispers something.
INT. JEAN'S ROOM - MORNING - RAIN
JEAN is seated rather regally. WILF, REGGIE and CISSY are standing just inside the door, before her. They exchange silent looks.
Please sit.

CISSY looks for a chair, vainly, and then sits on the edge of the bed. WILF, seeing no chair, up-ends a low wastepaperbin and sits on it as REGGIE goes around the bed and sits in the chair by the dresser. They sit.
(gestures) Wilf, you can sit on the bed.
You were the most brilliant Tosca ever, Jean. The last time I heard her Tosca, her vibrato sounded like it was waving at us.
Not waving, but drowning. Is that a yes?
INT. DINING ROOM - EVENING

Chatter. CISSY and WILF at their table. Sudden silence, then excited murmurs. Entering the dining room, JEAN on REGGIE'S arm making a royal entrance. They pass ANNE LANGLEY. JEAN gives her a gracious smile, a Queen Motherly wave. ANNE LANGLEY stares back, she gets up and marches out of the dining room. JEAN and REGGIE reach the table. As JEAN sits - One at a time, then joining all together, SIX RESIDENTS spread throughout the room start singing "Happy Birthday," as EVA and MARTA bring a lit Birthday cake towards OCTAVIA in her wheel chair.
Applause from the room as JEAN watches.
INT. ANNE LANGLEY'S ROOM - EVENING
ANNE LANGLEY stands up, furious. On the wall, posters with her name written in large type. Photographs, one of her, curtseying low, being presented to the Queen.
Pull yourself together, Anne. What does it matter when you sing?
ANNE LANGLEY is singing the aria from Tosca - Vissi D'Arte, accompanied by LETITIA.
INT. MAIN HALL - BACKSTAGE - DAY
Peeking between the curtains backstage, JEAN watches and listens to ANNE LANGLEY'S every move. !"#$%&'!"%('))%*%+',-.#'/0%*%1'2 !"#$%&'!"%('))%*%1'2
No. That's why I told him. (beat) And that was the mistake of my life. And now it's too late.
You still have your future.
I don't think there's a lot of it there just isn't. It's all been.
INT. COMMON ROOM - DAY

GEORGE, HARRY, TONY and DAVE finish "Underneath the Arches" to great applause. They bow, look at CEDRIC who is not applauding. They wave their hands to encourage him to clap. He does so. Limply. Encore! Encore!
I thought you'd never ask -
CEDRIC gives them a vulgar V sign as they go into 'RUN RABBIT RUN'.
INT. FITNESS ROOM & SMALL MUSIC ROOM - DAY
JEAN is now doing up the hooks on CISSY'S dress. JEAN struggles with CISSY'S hooks.
God, this is too tight for you, Cissy, I can't fasten itCISSY (becomes agitated)
But I altered it, I altered itA knock on the door and SIMON puts his head round.
It's getting near, ladies (calling) Did you hear that, gents, it's getting near? Thank you, Simon.
SIMON goes. JEAN still struggling. CISSY, more agitated.
